I am a PhD student in the Department of Agricultural and Resource Economics at Colorado State University. My research focuses on environmental and resource economics, agricultural conservation practices, climate risk, flood damages, crop insurance, and water resource economics.


My dissertation examines how agricultural conservation practices affect climate-related risks and environmental outcomes.

Cover crops and planting failure: How cover crop adoption affects prevented planting losses across regions, weather conditions, and cropping systems.
Conservation and flood damages: Whether upstream cover cropping practices reduce runoff, improve watershed hydrology, and mitigate downstream flood damages.
Conservation and property values: How reductions in flood risk and improvements in water quality affect housing markets and property values.

title: About Me
text:
I am currently a fifth-year PhD student in Agricultural and Resource Economics at Colorado State University. I plan to complete my preliminary dissertation defense in Fall 2026 and my final dissertation defense in Fall 2027.
Prior to joining Colorado State University, I earned a Master’s degree in Economics from the Institute of Business Administration (IBA), Karachi in Pakistan. My graduate training focused on econometrics, development economics, and international trade. I completed my undergraduate studies in Economics at Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, Pakistan.
During my PhD at Colorado State University, I have been involved in research assistantship roles. I was part of an interdisciplinary research project examining subsurface drip irrigation efficiency and deficit irrigation strategies for alfalfa production in Colorado, the Subsurface Irrigation Efficiency Project (SIEP). The project evaluated how irrigation management practices influence agricultural water use efficiency and production outcomes under water scarcity conditions.

title: Mutahir Sultan
text:
Current Research
The first chapter of my PhD dissertation examines how conservation practices, particularly cover crop adoption, affect planting failure risk in the United States, with a focus on heterogeneity across time and space. The second chapter studies the effects of agricultural conservation practices on flood damages, examining whether upstream cover cropping practices improve soil properties, reduce runoff, alter watershed hydrology, and mitigate downstream flood damages. The third chapter aims to investigate how reductions in flood risk and improvements in water quality resulting from agricultural conservation practices affect housing markets and property values.

title: Research
text:
Coauthors: Christopher Goemans and Dale Manning.
This paper examines the heterogeneous effects of cover crop adoption on prevented planting losses in the United States. Using multiple complementary datasets on cover crop adoption, producer-reported planted cover crop acreage, and high-resolution climate conditions, we analyze how the effects of cover crops vary across regions, weather conditions, and cropping systems. We find significant loss-mitigation effects in the Corn Belt, particularly under excessively wet planting-season conditions and within soybean production systems. The results suggest that improvements in soil conditions and field trafficability associated with cover cropping may play an important role in reducing planting failure risk under extreme weather conditions.



This project examines whether upstream agricultural conservation practices reduce downstream flood damages through improvements in watershed hydrology and runoff management.

Do Cover Crops Reduce Flood Damages?
Coauthors: Dale Manning and Gabriela Perez-Quesada.
This project examines whether upstream agricultural conservation practices reduce downstream flood damages through improvements in watershed hydrology and runoff management.

title: About Me
text:
Research Interests
My research interests are in applied microeconomics, environmental and resource economics, agricultural conservation policies, climate risk, flood damages, and crop insurance.
My current research primarily examines the causal effects of cover crop adoption on crop insurance losses and planting failure risk in U.S. agriculture. More broadly, my work studies how agricultural conservation practices influence watershed hydrology, runoff generation, and downstream flood damages. I am particularly interested in understanding the potential environmental externalities and risk-mitigation benefits associated with conservation agriculture under increasing climate variability.
